title: Replace per-customer compute provisioning with a 5-shape ML classifier
body: The Caprivane platform team adopted a tenant-classification model that bins each customer into one of five workload shapes (steady-low, steady-high, bursty-am, bursty-pm, weekly-spike) from their first 7 days of compute telemetry. Provisioning then targets the shape's p95 budget rather than the tenant's actual rolling p95. The bet is that 5 shapes capture >90% of tenant variation and that classification stability eliminates per-tenant tuning overhead.

assumptions.text: Five workload shapes capture more than 90% of tenant compute-pattern variation; the residual 10% can be hand-tuned without operational drag.
assumptions.outcome_evidence: Post-launch telemetry showed the bursty-pm shape contained two sub-populations (B2B-evening-batch and consumer-evening-spike) with p99 budgets differing by 3.2x. The five-shape model under-provisioned the consumer sub-population, producing latency-SLA breaches across 14 tenants. The residual 'unclassifiable' bucket grew to 23% of tenants within 8 weeks, far exceeding the 10% hand-tuning capacity assumed at adoption.

outcome.text: Six months in, the team rolled back to per-tenant rolling p95 with shape labels retained as a hint rather than a budget driver. Net cost savings from the classifier were less than the on-call cost of the SLA breach episodes.

title: Replace public 429 rate-limit responses with silent latency injection
body: The Frosthaven API team replaced public 429 Too Many Requests responses on rate-limited customer traffic with a shadow-throttle: requests above the limit return 200 OK after an artificially injected delay calibrated to the over-limit ratio. The argued benefit is fewer support tickets ('our integration is broken') and natural client backoff via observed slowness.

assumptions.text: Customers experiencing shadow throttle will adapt their request rate downward without escalating to support, because their integration appears to be working (just slower).
assumptions.outcome_evidence: Within four weeks the team observed a 1.6x rise in P0 escalations from enterprise customers complaining of 'random slowdowns' that they could not correlate with anything in their integration. The hidden cause prevented self-service tuning; clients with retry logic on timeout-bound paths instead amplified load (retried slow requests, multiplying upstream pressure). The mechanism the team relied on — natural rate-adaptation from observed slowness — never engaged because customers attributed slowness to platform faults, not their own over-limit traffic.

outcome.text: Public 429s reinstated for over-quota traffic; shadow-throttle reserved for short bursts within tolerance. Documentation re-emphasized rate-limit observability as a customer-facing surface.

title: Add EUR alongside USD in the legacy invoicing system
body: The Lumeyra billing team is adding EUR support to the legacy invoicing system (built ~2009) without rewriting the core invoice generator. The plan rests on the team's audit finding that hardcoded currency assumptions are concentrated in the display / formatting layer and the core data layer is currency-agnostic.

assumptions.text: Hardcoded currency assumptions in the legacy invoicing system are concentrated in the display / formatting layer; the data, tax, and reconciliation layers are currency-agnostic and require no schema or logic changes for EUR.
assumptions.outcome_evidence: Discovered post-launch: the tax-rounding subroutine had USD-specific cents-precision baked in, producing per-line-item rounding mismatches against EUR's decimal-handling expectations. Worse, the credit-note workflow relied on a USD-implicit comparison in the dispute-resolution function that silently classified EUR credits as outside-tolerance, blocking automated reconciliation. The currency-agnostic claim was true at the SCHEMA level but false at the BUSINESS-LOGIC level — currency assumptions had leaked into financial calculations independently of the explicit currency-denomination columns. ~6 weeks of forensic accounting required to retroactively fix mis-issued credit notes.

outcome.text: EUR support shipped after a second round of work that introduced explicit currency-routing in tax + reconciliation. Schema-only audit replaced with a behavior-test sweep before any future currency addition.

title: Detect bot traffic via mouse-movement entropy on signup forms
body: The Verdant Trust security team adopted a client-side bot detector that measures mouse-movement entropy during signup form interaction. The bet: human mouse-movement is high-entropy and distinguishable from automated clicks (low entropy, straight-line trajectories, exact pixel-precision) at p<0.05 with 200ms of observation. The detector blocks signups that score below the threshold.

assumptions.text: 200ms of mouse-movement observation is enough to distinguish human users from automated bots at p<0.05; sub-threshold signups are reliably automated.
assumptions.outcome_evidence: Bot-driven signups did not collapse — sophisticated bots adopted Bezier-curve mouse simulation libraries within 3 weeks of the detector's launch and crossed the entropy threshold trivially. Worse, false-positive rate on legitimate signups using touchscreens (~31% of mobile traffic) ran at 6.4% — touchscreen events appeared as discrete taps without intervening mouse movement, scoring below the human-entropy threshold despite being human. Net effect: marginal bot reduction (~12%) at substantial human-friction cost (~6.4% legitimate signups blocked).

outcome.text: Detector retired after 8 weeks. Replaced with server-side rate-shape + reputation-based scoring; touchscreen path explicitly carved out.

title: Auto-tune observability sampling rate per service from a 7-day prior
body: The Wexham platform observability team rolled out an auto-tuner that sets per-service span-sampling rates from the prior 7 days of telemetry. The optimization target: minimize storage cost while keeping p99-latency error under 5%. The bet is that 7 days of prior data is statistically sufficient for the optimizer to converge on a stable per-service rate without operator input.

assumptions.text: Seven days of prior telemetry is statistically sufficient for the optimizer to converge on a stable per-service sampling rate; weekly variation is small enough that the resulting rate generalizes forward.
assumptions.outcome_evidence: Three weeks post-launch, an unrelated marketing campaign caused a 12x traffic spike on a previously-low-volume service; the optimizer (still using pre-spike rolling window) under-sampled by ~40x for the high-traffic period and produced p99 estimates with confidence intervals so wide that on-call engineers stopped trusting the dashboard. Convergence held for steady-state services, but discovery of new failure modes during traffic shifts (the most operationally-load-bearing time) was the WORST regime for the auto-tuner. The 'weekly variation is small' claim was true on average but false in the tail.

outcome.text: Auto-tuner constrained to operate only on services with stable 30-day rolling traffic profiles; high-volatility services reverted to operator-set rates. The shift from full automation to constrained-domain automation reduced the savings claim by ~60%.
